speaker: interviewee
text: [TEST PATTERN ALARM]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is April 14, 1996. The survivor is Fred Wolf, the interviewer, Robin Rundle. City, state, Santa Monica, California, United States of America. And the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Robin Rundle. Today's date is April 14, 1996. I have the privilege of interviewing Fred Wolf today, in Santa Monica, California, in the United States of America, for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Can you give your name for us and spell it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Manfred Wolf, M-A-N-F-R-E-D W-O-L-F.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my name at birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. I adopted Fred and Wolf after I made my citizenship in this country. I didn't want, didn't want to give up that Manfred. It would, would be important for my restitution.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you go by-- tell us what you go by now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go by Fred M. Wolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you state your birth date for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on July 15, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today I'm 72.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and spelling and country of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Merl on the Mosel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Merl is M-E-R-L. A-D means on the Mosel, what's the river who runs right by Merl. It's in Germany, and it's [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: In the district of Koblenz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Its, it's the district of Koblenz. [GERMAN] means the district for this Merl and the surrounding little towns, villages, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the city name was Merl ad Mosel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can we begin by you describing what your pre-war home life was like-- how many people there were in your household, and who they were, what your house looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK We had a three-story home. My father, my mother, my grandmother, and in the beginning, as I can remember, was a aunt. Her name was Claire or Clara. She stayed there till she later got married into a different township, not far from, from Merl. But it's on the Luxembourg German border. The name of the little town was Bollendorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name and your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Edward Wolf. My mother's name was Ricka Wolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was [? Frankel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother's name was Johanna Wolf. But now I do not remember her maiden name. The only way to find out is go to the cemetery and get it from there. I haven't-- I can't remember that far. Hopefully, I will be there this year and probably photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like for you growing up? Can you describe a daily routine, an average day in your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I was still a child without going to school, I woke up in the morning, well, until they came to me and, Manfred, get up! So I get up, do your, your regular chores-- washing, brushing your teeth. And then I went out. And the summertime was very nice. Next to my home was a man who made wine barrels. And I always helped there, looked how he did. And I also helped to shave the, the, the boards who go into this wine barrel. The wine barrel kept about 1,000 liters of wine. And it was interesting how they formed this into a barrel from a straight piece of wood. From that was one thing I do remember. There's others-- neighbors, children we played. There was a locksmith-- not a locksmith, what they call a blacksmith. I used to like to go to him and see how he fit the, the, no, the horse shoes to the horse. I helped him pulling on the, on the-- I don't know how they call this in English-- to, to steer the heat. And he had the, the, the, no, the horseshoe in that, in that place to heat it up real hot, white red, so it could be moved and bent so it would fit to the hooves of the, of the horse. I always-- sometimes, it had a funny smell to it. I don't know why, I liked that smell. [LAUGHS] It's a funny thing, but-- I don't know. Many children here have never had the chance to see that, I guess. But it was just a small town, 1,500 inhabitants. I was the only Jewish child there. There was one more Jewish family in this town. But it had no, no-- they had children, but they were much older than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was interested even in sports. I can remember that the, the town had a-- what they call? Where they gather together, like right next to the railroad station was a small station. And, and they-- what is the name? In fall, there was a place where they-- the corn came in, and it was-- what do they call that? They, they-- no. The, the, the corn with, with the stingers, everything was thrown in the top. And on one side came the corn itself. Out of the other side, it was bundled up as straw. They used this facility there for all kinds of, even the circus came, came there and had their doings-- sports events. I can remember at one time they built a little groove in the, in the ground and for white jumping. How do they call this in English? Fast jumping. So I was just little. Probably by that time I was already 10 or 11. And everybody jumped. And there was sawdust, naturally, in the jumping area. And they have a board, a little stick across, how far everybody could jump. And I-- as eager as I was, I didn't want to give up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can remember a day later, I couldn't walk. My, my, my muscles, everything was so stiff that my father just laughed. And he said, well, we get you a couple of crutches. They'll go away after a few days. And so it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there some sports that your father enjoyed that you participated in with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, no, not directly, but he was much older. I wasn't a bowler. But he went bowling. And he went to auto racing. And I only stood home, listened on the radio who is going to win. Mercedes Benz or [NON-ENGLISH] or Maserati and Bugatti. All those, all those cars, Italians and German cars, were racing. And the, the name of the, of the racetrack was called Nurburgring.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was close to your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- I don't know. It took them probably by bus maybe three, four hours. They started early in the morning to get there in time. That Nurburgring is still there. It's not far from-- in the vicinity of Bitburg. Probably everybody knows and heard about Bitburg when Reagan was up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father like to swim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he liked to swim, naturally. That, that was another thing that he, he showed me how not to be afraid. He got a few meters or yards up the coast, jumped in, and swam down the river and encouraged me to do the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go that far. But he, whatever he did, he let me swim alongside of him to learn how to make strokes-- breaststrokes and other strokes. And that's what I liked about him. The opposite was my mother, who did not-- whenever we went swimming, she took a cord, a string, and put it around my, my wrist. And she stood on the, on the line, on the outside from the river, and let me go in as long as that, that string lasted. So I felt that I was, I was not, I was not free enough to, to do what I wanted. But she was a mother. She was afraid that something could happen. And a lot of, a lot of-- many times, people drowned in that river. And I know a good friend who I, who lived just a couple doors from me drowned there. And they-- I seen it happen and I screamed from, from the, from the outside. I was stood on the, on top of the railroad tracks, who are much higher. And I hollered and I screamed to people that-- I forgot his name-- that he's drowning, drowning, drowning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people come running and trying to get him out. But it was too late. And your mother was fearful for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, naturally, from that, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your schooling. Where did you first go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to school in a kindergarten. There was a, there was a hospital, and it was run by nuns. They also had a kindergarten. So I am, like everybody else, went to the kindergarten. Or I have no idea, not everybody went there. But, naturally, it wasn't done for free. I know that my father, many times when there was clothes and anything left over in the store, he sent me to go and bring it to the sisters to, you know, show the appreciation. They knew who to give it to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I spent there until about maybe till 4 o'clock or so. And I was actually brought up just like any Catholic down there. I was the only Jew. But I'm not-- didn't want to stand out. So I did everything what they did, what the children did. Even you make your cross and you sit down and you pray. And that's all, and nobody questions me. Even my heart, I knew what I was inside, naturally. But they, they took care of me, and there was no question asked. Tell us about the religious observances followed in your home, and how did you know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the, the important thing was, or whatever it was that I was Jewish, was because we kept kosher. If people know, we didn't-- my grandmother was very strict in that way. And when, when the meat we ate or whatever we ate, we kept kosher. That means if you eat meat, you have to wait two hours before you can eat milk things. But the other way around is when you eat milk, that only takes a half an hour or so, and you can eat meat. It's, you know, I mean, it's a surprise. I still remember that. [LAUGHS] But that was part of this, the separation from in the house and what was outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do to celebrate the Sabbath? Can you describe what happened in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Sabbath? Well, the synagogue was in next, in the next village. Well, next little town was a little bigger town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Zell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-E-L-L, Zell on the Mosel, on the Mosel. There was a, there was a synagogue. And my father and I, on high holidays, we walked. If it wasn't that high of a holiday, we took our bikes and we rode, which was against the law too. You shouldn't, you shouldn't even ride anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the synagogue was a-- I don't know, it was, it was not strict-- how should I say-- strict orthodox. I think it, it was in between. The woman had to be up on the balcony and wear little things on their head. And men could only stay downstairs. And the men were not allowed to go upstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was just a child. Many times I sneaked up. My mother was there. My grandmother was there. You know, we talked, or we schmoozed, like they say. And then they told me, well, Manfred, now it's time. Go down. The Torah is going to be lifted out. And it's very sacred to observe that with seriousness. Well, that-- till the end of the session, at the end of the service, everybody went out. We went home. And we went home to, to eat. And there was another thing that my grandma was very strict in. There was no cooking going on on Saturday on Shabbat. The cooking was done on Friday. And there was a special kugel they're called, potato kugel or whatever. And it was put on Friday night in the oven. And kept-- the coals kept warm all through the night until the next day. So we did not have to light up anything like the coals or anything. We just took it out and served it on the table, without doing anything wrong. And when they came to Saturday night, when the week was over, and what I don't forget-- [CRYING] My grandmother always blessed me like this. She had a, she had a candle. It was twisted, with a lot of colors on it. And she took, she took a little dish, poured wine in it. And the candle was lit. And then she put the candle into the wine and lit it-- I mean, and extinguished it. And then she said, well-- how they say-- have a good week. [HEBREW], they say that in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she would do this every week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were her only grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- you know, this still goes with me. [SOBBING] Sometimes I feel that this helped me in life to stay on the earth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, tell us about other religious observances.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you mean-- well, we had Passover, there was Hanukkah, there was, there was Yom Kippur and, and Rosh Hashanah, naturally. We observed that very, very much. On Rosh Hashanah we all had went to the temple, to the synagogue. And on Yom Kippur, nobody ate nothing. That means-- I was the only child. Until my Bar Mitzvah, I could go along with a half a day. After a half a day, they sent me home and said, OK, you can go home. There's something to eat there. You're OK. But I will not forget, a year before my Bar Mitzvah, I was told to fast a whole day. And my father said, yes, you have to fast. There's no buts about it. And when it came about 5 o'clock in the afternoon, I couldn't stand it. I had to go out, and I threw up. But my father go, oh, that's fine, that's OK. You can wait here until the service is over, and we all go together home. And you'll, you'll be OK by the next day, don't worry about it. [LAUGHS] He guided me [SOB] very much that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he went hiking--

speaker: interviewee
text: We, yes, well, there was days, especially on a Sunday, when anybody else, you know, everybody else went to church there. And Sunday we did not work, and he did not work either. So we went out hiking. After we climbed up past the grapes, we were in the woods. And summertime, we had, we could pick wild strawberries and wild blackberries. And he always looked for the clover leaves and looked for a four clover leaf.

speaker: interviewer
text: The four-leaf clover.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four-leaf clover who's supposed to bring luck. And that's what he always looked for. And then we, we came walking back. And that was like a Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much interaction with your neighbors, since you were one of two Jewish families in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was, there was sometimes things that the children always looked at me like I'm the Jew. OK. They knew that. And sometimes they come up with those stupid things, like you killed Jesus, things like that. And I just was buffed at those things, naturally, in the beginning. And my father said-- they started fighting. And I was only a little guy, and three or four guys tried to gang up on me. And we had, we had two entrances. Actually, the main entrance was on the house. And then there was another one. It was like a cellar and had steel doors. And he, he told me, get out, fight. Don't, don't be a chicken. Don't be-- you have to show your-- get out. They're not going to do nothing. But if you back up, then they come after you. But if you show you are not afraid, they will stay away from you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It worked. But still, it wasn't an easy thing. OK. Sometimes it worked, sometimes, you know, they ganged up. And I got inside and I locked this steel door. And they pounded on it, you know. Then when they seen I'm gone, I'm not coming out anymore, they walked away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience antisemitism from adults as well as children?

speaker: interviewee
text: From adults, not as much. They, they, they did not show that directly. They might have showed it later. That was before 1933. But once Hitler got elected, and after 1933, then they too showed a dislike. Because the propaganda was so big that-- and the lies, whatever was told and tacked about the Jews, what they are, so on, so on, they had looked at me also down and tried to tell me, get away, get away from here, you Jew. And I know that my father used to go to a barber. And he-- the barber, he never shaved by himself, the funny thing about that. And the barber shaved him with a straight razor. And he went there two, three, I don't know how many times a week. I got my hair cut there too. And then one day, that was after 1933 or '35, the barber told him, Ed-- they called him Ed, Edward, Ed-- I don't think, I can't do this anymore. I cannot take-- let you come in anymore. I mean, I like you and I love you. We know each other for so many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the neighborhood, the [GERMAN]. You know what the [GERMAN] is? The Nazi-- how should I say [GERMAN]. It's a, it's the ones who belong to the, to the Hitler-- Party.

speaker: interviewee
text: Party, there you go. [GERMAN] is party. It's so close. [LAUGHS] So they told me you cannot come into me anymore. So that was little by little, the thing started to go. And I can remember that there was a paper, a newspaper, it's called And it was published from one of the biggest Nazis in the government. And it showed the real ugly side of Jewish people, with noses overdrawn. And what they did, killing our children or, or cheating on this and that, that they are bad. And if they are gone, then for us, the, the, the living will be so much better. And there's one thing, that every time when they're marched-- and there was some kind of a festival with them. So they marched, and around, around the homes, around the street. And then when they came to our home, they stopped. And then they start singing [GERMAN]-- no-- [GERMAN] What meant, [GERMAN], it's like this also. I forgot about that. When the knife, when the knife, from the Jewish blood runs off the knife, then things for us will go twice as good.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're coming to the end of tape number one, interview with Fred Wolf.
